summary.en: This is the heart of Cape May's postcard image, a living museum of 19th-century architecture with gas lamps, intricate "gingerbread" trim, and horse-drawn carriages.
This area is where manicured charm gives way to wilder nature and maritime industry, featuring windswept dunes, military history, and a major commercial fishing port.
Just beyond the historic district, this separate borough has a distinctly bohemian and agricultural soul with a slower pace, sprawling farms, and quirky independent shops.

description.en: A charming, authentic French bakery perfect for a morning espresso and a flaky croissant before a day of architectural admiration.
A no-frills, old-school local's haunt where the beer is cold and the conversation is genuine, offering a feel for the town's year-round pulse.
A Cape May institution famous for its brunch, served on the sun-dappled veranda of the Carroll Villa Hotel.
A fantastic, casual spot for authentic tacos and burritos, providing a delicious and speedy break from the dominant American and seafood scene.
Located on the Washington Street Mall, Tisha's offers an elegant but comfortable fine-dining experience known for its classic American cuisine.
This powerful museum explores the life of the famed abolitionist and her deep connections to Cape May, where she worked to fund her Underground Railroad missions.
A stunning 1879 Stick Style mansion that offers a fantastic look into Victorian life, with eccentric architecture best admired from its grounds.
Wander Hughes, Gurney, and Columbia Avenues to see an open-air museum of Victorian homes with intricate, whimsical, and colorful wooden trim.
An acclaimed professional theater housed in a stunningly preserved 1853 church, a landmark worth admiring even without seeing a show.
A beloved, no-nonsense dockside tavern surrounded by commercial fishing boats, this is the spot for a cold beer and a dozen fresh oysters.
A legendary outdoor beach bar with sand floors, fire pits, and live music, it is the undisputed champion of sunset drinks.
A sprawling Cape May institution right on the harbor, famous for its multiple dining rooms and dockside Raw Bar.
A small, family-run market where you can get the freshest local catch, like steamed shrimp and cocktail sauce for an unmatched beach picnic.
The last of its kind in New Jersey, this 7-story concrete tower offers a breathtaking 360-degree view of the peninsula.
Famous for its spectacular sunsets over the Delaware Bay and the 'Cape May diamonds'—naturally polished quartz pebbles that wash ashore.
A bizarre, skeletal wreck of an experimental WWI-era vessel, visible just off the coast of Sunset Beach since a storm in 1926.
A world-renowned birding destination with trails through marsh and dunes, offering a wilder side of the Jersey Shore.
An iconic 1859 lighthouse located within Cape May Point State Park, offering stunning views for those who climb to the top.
A beloved local deli, gourmet market, and community hub, perfect for grabbing a fantastic made-to-order sandwich and a coffee.
A culinary destination disguised as a country market, known for inventive, high-end cuisine in a completely unpretentious setting.
Enjoy a phenomenal farm-to-table breakfast or lunch from the kitchen of this 62-acre working farm, offering a true taste of the Garden State.
Wander the grounds of this stunning 62-acre working farm, visit with the chickens and pigs, and see where produce for Cape May's best restaurants is grown.
A coastal birding habitat with peaceful walking trails through dunes and wetlands, offering a sanctuary for birds and people alike.
Keep an eye out for lima bean motifs on signs and in shops, celebrating West Cape May's proud, quirky claim as the 'Lima Bean Capital of the World'.
Experience the area by walking or biking the quiet back roads connecting the main village to surrounding farms, enjoying a unique landscape of open fields.
Explore the various farm stands that dot the landscape, offering fresh, local produce and a taste of the area's agricultural soul.

prompt.en: Find the most intricate piece of 'gingerbread' trim on a Victorian home and take a close-up photo of its details.
As dusk settles, capture a photo of a street scene illuminated by the warm, historic glow of a gas lamp.
Find a 'painted lady' house with at least five distinct colors and take a photo that showcases its vibrant color scheme.
Take a photo of an inviting porch on one of the historic inns or homes. Extra points for a rocking chair or a swing!
Capture a photo of a street scene from a low angle, imagining the view from a horse-drawn carriage.
Get a classic seaside treat like ice cream or fudge and take a selfie with a beautiful Victorian building in the background.
Find a modern object (like a car, a sign, or a bicycle) and photograph it contrasting sharply with the 19th-century architecture.
Peer past a gate or down an alley to find and photograph a hidden garden or charming courtyard.
Find a historical marker or plaque on a building and take a photo of it. What story does it tell?
Capture the essence of a Cape May brunch. Photograph your meal on a sun-dappled porch or patio.
Photograph an interesting window display on the Washington Street Mall that feels like a glimpse into another time.
Step off the main mall onto a quiet side street and capture a scene that feels authentic and lived-in.
Capture the classic, spectacular sunset over the Delaware Bay. Try to include the silhouette of the concrete ship or a person.
Find a detail that tells the story of the working harbor. Take a close-up photo of fishing nets, thick ropes, or a weathered boat hull.
Find your best 'Cape May diamond' or a unique seashell and take an artistic photo of it in the sand.
Take a windswept selfie on the beach or a jetty, with the ocean or bay stretching out behind you.
Photograph the World War II Lookout Tower from a low angle to emphasize its height and solitary watch over the coast.
Take a close-up, abstract photo of a natural texture: the pattern of dune grass, the ripples in the sand, or the surface of a weathered piece of driftwood.
Capture a delicious-looking photo of your fresh seafood meal, whether it's oysters at a tavern or steamed shrimp on the beach.
Find a spot where the fishing boats are reflected in the harbor water and capture the colorful, distorted image.
Without disturbing them, try to photograph a shorebird along the water's edge or in the state park's marshes.
Take a photo looking straight up the side of the Cape May Lighthouse from its base.
If you're near a beach bar at night, capture the cozy, warm glow of a fire pit with friends.
From a high vantage point like the tower, try to capture the line where the Atlantic Ocean meets the Delaware Bay.
Take a vibrant, colorful photo of fresh produce at a farm stand or market. Focus on the colors and textures.
Find a quiet back road and take a photo looking down its length, capturing the peaceful, rural atmosphere.
Find something that captures the quirky, bohemian vibe of the borough—a funky sign, a unique mailbox, or a colorful shop front.
If you're exploring by bike, take a selfie during a break with a farm or open field in the background.
Find any reference to a lima bean—on a sign, a menu, or in a shop—and snap a photo of it.
In the late afternoon, capture the warm, golden light filtering across one of the open farm fields.
Photograph your farm-fresh meal or gourmet sandwich from one of the local markets or eateries.
Take a photo of a classic farm building, like a white barn or a rustic shed, against the sky.
Capture a peaceful, quiet moment within the South Cape May Meadows Preserve. Focus on the reeds, the water, or the trail ahead.
Respectfully photograph one of the farm animals, like the chickens or pigs, in their environment.
Find a wildflower growing on the side of a country lane or trail and take a macro shot of it.
Take a wide landscape photo that emphasizes the vast, open sky over the flat farm fields.
